coredata.dc:title: Facile, gram-scale and eco-friendly synthesis of multi-color graphene quantum dots by thermal-driven advanced oxidation process
coredata.dc:creator: Lyu, Bowen
Li, Hui-Jun
Xue, Fengfeng
Sai, Liman
Gui, Bojie
Qian, Dongjin
Wang, Xianying
Yang, Junhe
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been demonstrated of great potential and benefits in the fields of bioimaging and white light-emitting-diodes (WLEDs). However, it is still highly demanding at the current level to solve the dilemma of achieving high-yield GQDs of good quality and superior fluorescent property using low-cost sustainable and industrializable production procedure. In this work, we for the first time report the gram-scale synthesis of well-crystalline GQDs with ultra-small size based on thermal-driven Advanced Oxidation Process (AOP) under facile green hydrothermal conditions. The average yield calculated from 20 trials reached up to 60%, and the average size of the dots was measured to be ~3.7 nm. Furtherly, GQDs with the photoluminescence (PL) emission of blue, green, yellow, orange, and red have been prepared by expanding the π-conjugation and introducing graphite nitrogen in the carbon skeleton based on chemical structure engineering. The PL-tunable GQDs have an average size distribution of 2–5 nm and a lamellar structure of 2–6 layers. Structure analysis results have indicated that the red shift of PL emission is attributed to bandgap narrowing. This approach successfully converts the easily available and cheap precursor into high-valued products with great application potentials. The PL-tunable GQDs have been successfully used as fluorescent probes of good biocompatibility for in vitro/ in vivo bio-imaging and to produce highly-photostable white-light-emitting composite film with a quantum yield (QY) of 24%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Advanced oxidation process
Bandgap engineering
Bioimaging
White light-emitting film

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of oxidized GQDs via AOP
Preparation of a white-light-emitting composite film
Bioimaging and white light-emitting composite film

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology and structural characterization of F-GQDs and graphite oxide. (a) TEM image, scale bar, 20 nm. The inset is the corresponding lateral size distribution. (b) HR-TEM image, scale bar, 2 nm. (c) AFM image, scale bar, 20 nm. The inset is the height profile along the red line. (d) XRD patterns, (e) Raman spectra, (f) FT-IR spectra, (g) UV–vis absorbance spectra and (h) PL decay spectra and fitting curves of graphite oxides and F-GQD.

TEM images, AFM images and particle size distribution diagrams of PL-tunable GQDs, from left to right are: B-, G-, Y-, O-, and R-GQDs. (The inset is the height profile along the green line and the corresponding HRTEM images).
(a–e) UV–vis absorbance spectra, PL spectra and photographs (inset) of B-, G-, Y-, O-, and R-GQDs under UV-light irradiation. (f) PL decay spectra and fitting curves of the five GQDs.

(a) Cathodic linear sweep voltammetry and (b) anodic linear sweep voltammetry of B-, G-, Y-, O-, and R-GQDs. The horizontal intercept of tangent line in (a) or (b) determines the CBM or VBM of each GQD type, respectively. (c) Illustration of the energy level diagram of PL-tunable GQDs.

Confocal fluorescence images and the corresponding brightfield images of HepG2 cells treated with (a, d) B-GQDs at a single l = 405 nm laser excitation, (b, e) G-GQDs at a single l = 488 nm laser excitation, and (c, f) R-GQDs at a single l = 559 nm laser excitation. Scale bar, 35 μm. PL images of a mouse (22–25 g) (g) before and after subcutaneous injection of 100 μL of the O-GQDs (h) and R-GQDs (i) aqueous solution (1 mg/mL). The excitation wavelength is 530 nm.

(a) PL spectrum, (b) Photostability of white-GQDs/PVA film under UV light non-stop irradiation for 24 h (150 W Xe lamp with a center wavelength of 420 nm). F0 and F are the PL intensities at 0 h and a specific time, respectively. (c) CIE colour coordinate of the GQDs/PVA composite film emitting white light under UV irradiation. The inset in (a) is a photograph of the white light-emitting GQDs solution and the corresponding film under UV irradiation.
Schematic of stripping graphite oxide flakes into GQDs.
A brief summary of GQD preparation approaches.
Percentage breakdown of different bonds within the XPS high-resolution spectra calculated from Fig. S7.

head.ce:title: Facile, gram-scale and eco-friendly synthesis of multi-color graphene quantum dots by thermal-driven advanced oxidation process

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Shanghai for Science and Technology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of High-Performance Ceramics and Superfine Microstructure

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Shanghai Normal University, Guilin Road 100, Shanghai 200234, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Shanghai Key Laboratory of Molecular Catalysis and Innovative Materials, Fudan University, No. 220 Handan Rd., Shanghai 200433, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Molecular Catalysis and Innovative Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Shanghai Key Laboratory of Molecular Catalysis and Innovative Materials, Fudan University, No. 220 Handan Rd., Shanghai 200433, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Bowen Lyu and Hui-Jun Li contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been demonstrated of great potential and benefits in the fields of bioimaging and white light-emitting-diodes (WLEDs). However, it is still highly demanding at the current level to solve the dilemma of achieving high-yield GQDs of good quality and superior fluorescent property using low-cost sustainable and industrializable production procedure. In this work, we for the first time report the gram-scale synthesis of well-crystalline GQDs with ultra-small size based on thermal-driven Advanced Oxidation Process (AOP) under facile green hydrothermal conditions. The average yield calculated from 20 trials reached up to 60%, and the average size of the dots was measured to be ~3.7 nm. Furtherly, GQDs with the photoluminescence (PL) emission of blue, green, yellow, orange, and red have been prepared by expanding the π-conjugation and introducing graphite nitrogen in the carbon skeleton based on chemical structure engineering. The PL-tunable GQDs have an average size distribution of 2–5 nm and a lamellar structure of 2–6 layers. Structure analysis results have indicated that the red shift of PL emission is attributed to bandgap narrowing. This approach successfully converts the easily available and cheap precursor into high-valued products with great application potentials. The PL-tunable GQDs have been successfully used as fluorescent probes of good biocompatibility for in vitro/ in vivo bio-imaging and to produce highly-photostable white-light-emitting composite film with a quantum yield (QY) of 24%.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) are excellent zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with unique optical properties and good biocompatibility [1–3]. As a member of the graphene family, GQDs have superior electrical conductivity, and their bandgap can be adjusted by modulating the size due to quantum confinement effects. Their extremely stable fluorescence, tunable photoluminescence emissions and high color purity has offered many advantages for replacing toxic light-conversion inorganic quantum dots [4–7], or unstable fluorescent dyes in optical devices. For the present, various researches have been focused on preparing multi-color GQDs, which have been studied emphasizedly for biological fluorescent probes and white light-emitting devices (WLEDs) [8–10]. Xiong et al. prepared PL-tunable carbon dots (CDs) of bright full-color emissions from blue to near-infrared region, which are successfully applied in the fields of cell and even in vivo imaging [8]. Fan et al. synthesized multi-color luminescent CDs, and the Lmax of the blue-light dot-based devices could reach ~136 cdm−2 which is the best reported performance of carbon quantum dots-based monochromatic electroluminescent LEDs [9]. These studies fully demonstrate the potential and benefits of GQDs as efficient fluorescent materials in high-performance optical devices and applications.

So far, versatile synthetic methods and diverse precursors have been attempted to prepare GQDs. Among these traditional preparation methods cutting sp2-carbon materials, strong oxidizing agents and acids (e.g., NaClO3, KMnO4, HNO3, and H2SO4) are frequently used for stripping precursors. In spite of its high efficiency, these cutting reagents are extremely dangerous and the accompanying environmental pollution caused by the reaction by-products including inorganic salts and acids has undoubtfully increased the difficulty of operation and also preparation cost. Besides, the cutting methods can hardly guarantee achieving GQDs with uniform size, due to the restriction of reaction conditions. To solve the hurdles, new environmental-friendly shearing agents have been exploited successively to improve the cutting efficiency and simplify the operation process [11–16]. Zhou et al. prepared GQDs of large size with graphene oxide as the precursor and
ClO 3 -
as the cutting agent under UV irradiation [11]. Fan et al. reported an electrochemical exfoliation method by immersing graphite rods in K2S2O8 solution by using
S 2
O 8
2 -
as the cutting agent [12]. Furtherly, Yang et al. used W18O49 nanowire as a catalyst to shear graphene oxides into GQDs [14]. Zhou et al. have attempted UV-Fenton reaction to prepare GQDs of large-size (~40 nm) for DNA detection [15,16]. However, these strategies generally prepared GQDs from graphene oxides which are expensive raw materials, and it still remains difficult to achieve gram-scale synthesis of GQDs with high crystalline quality, ultra-small uniform size and super fluorescent property at one time. Therefore, how to enhance the yield of GQDs with superior optical performance utilizing low-cost sustainable and industrializable methods becomes highly demanding and particularly important.

Besides, precise regulation of the luminescent properties of GQDs is greatly desired for practical importance. As common in the investigation of a fluorescent material, systematic tuning of the spectral property and energy structure of the GQDs based on chemical unit engineering has attracted a great interest. To finely control the optical properties of GQDs, synergistic strategies to systematically tailor the structure of GQDs have been developed, including tuning the bandgap (separation between the highest occupied molecular orbital and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital), and surface states [8,9,17–19]. Specifically, bandgap narrowing could be realized by enlarging π conjugated sp2-carbon systems or introducing an intermediate n-orbital via surface modification. In 2012, Tetsuka et al. used graphene oxide and ammonia to prepare amino-functionalized GQDs, and firstly discovered the importance of size regulation and nitrogen doping in bandgap tuning [18]. In 2018, Yan et al. prepared GQDs modified with various polyaromatic rings to expand the π-conjugate structure, successfully achieving efficient band gap modulation and application for CO2 reduction [17]. Despite the tremendous strategies, there still is a great challenge to systematically study the PL mechanism for precisely controlling the optical properties of GQDs. Actually, the aim of expanding the applying scope of GQDs determines the necessity of controllable bandgap tuning for multi-color fluorescence, which requires deeper mechanism research.

Herein, an improved thermal-drvien advanced oxidation process (AOP) has been utilized for the first time to achieve high-yield synthesis of well-crystalline GQDs. The AOP reagent could provide abundant strong oxidizer which could shear cheap graphene oxides into GQDs with uniform and small size. The AOP-driven cutting strategy is environmental-friendly by avoiding the use of strong acids, and the reaction by-products could be easily removed via simple filtrate. The formation mechanism of the GQDs via stripping the graphite oxides has been researched. Furtherly, to develop more possibilities in the application field, nitrogen-doping of the GQDs via modification with different nitrogen resources was accomplished, which successfully and precisely manipulate the band structures and fluorescent properties of GQDs. To study the PL mechanism, the band structures of different GQDs were then calculated via utilizing linear sweep voltammetry to determine the conduction band minimum (CBM) and valence band maximum (VBM). Based on the novel and excellent fluorescent property, the PL-tunable GQDs have been demonstrated as a fluorescent probe in in vitro/in vivo bioimage and used for preparing a white-light-emitting composite film.

All the chemicals are commercially available and used without further purification. Graphite oxide powders were purchased from The Sixth Element (Changzhou) Materials Technology Co., Ltd. M-Phenylenediamine, o-Phenylenediamine, p-Phenylenediamine were purchased from A J&K Scientific Technology Co., Ltd. Ethylenediamine (EDA) were purchased from Sigma Co., Ltd. Quinine sulfate, rhodamine 6G and rhodamine B (RhB) were purchased from Shanghai Lanji Technology Development Co., Ltd. Polyvinyl alcohol (Mw = 96500), from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). The HepG2 cells were provided by the Institute of Biochemistry and Cell Biology, China.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of oxidized GQDs via AOP
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 150 mL of graphite oxide powders (GO) aqueous solution (15 mg/mL) was added into 20 mL of H2O2 aqueous solution (10.0 M, 30 wt%), followed by addition of 25 mg FeCl3. The mixture was transferred to a 200-mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. To purify the product, the obtained yellow solution was filtered by 0.22 μm nylon membrane, followed by freeze-drying. Finally, yellow powder was obtained in an overall yield of 60%. The oxidized GQDs powder could be re-dispersed in water, and the solution could remain stable for several months without any precipitation. This sample was named as F-GQDs.

In a typical procedure for extracting blue, green, yellow, orange, red graphene quantum dots (B-, G-, Y-, O-, R-GQDs), the F-GQDs aqueous solution (5 mL, 2 mg/mL) was mixed with 10 mg m-Phenylenediamine (mpd), ethylenediamine, o-phenylenediamine (opd) and p-phenylenediamine (ppd) in 15 mL of deionized water, respectively. After stirring for 30 min, the mixture was transferred to a 50-mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. The obtained solution was dialyzed with cellulose ester dialysis mem-brane (MWCO 100–500 Da) to remove excessive organic molecules. Finally, the solution was concentrated by a rotary evaporator and freeze-dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of a white-light-emitting composite film
ce:section.ce:para: For a white-light-emitting film, a precursor solution was firstly prepared by mixing B-GQDs, G-GQDs and R-GQDs in a weight ratio of 2:2:3. The white-light-emitting solution (1.0 mg/mL) was then mixed with 10 mL of PVA aqueous solution (0.15 g/mL). The mixture was added dropwise to a clean grinder and vacuum-dried at 60 °C for 1 day.

The HepG2 cells provided by the Institute of Biochemistry and Cell Biology, China were grown in RPMI 1640 media, supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2 at 37 °C.

The Cytotoxicity of the GQDs was assessed by performing MTT assay with the HepG2 cells. Cells were seeded into a 96-well plate at 2 × 103/well and were cultured at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 24 h. Different concentrations of GQDs (0, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400 and 500 µg/mL) were added to different wells. After incubation for 12 or 24 h, the MTT (0.5 mg/mL) was added to each well, and the plate was incubated for another 4 h. The optical densities at 490 nm of the GQDs were measured.

Cells were plated on 14 mm glass coverslips and incubated overnight. The cells were incubated with 100 µg/mL B-GQDs, G-GQDs and R-GQDs in RPMI 1640 for 3 h at 37 °C, and washed with RPMI 1640 media. After being washed for three times, the cells were subjected to CLSM imaging with excitation wavelengths of 405, 488 and 559 nm, respectively.

The animal protocols were approved to be appropriate and humane by the institutional animal care and use committee of the Chinese Academy of Sciences. Animal experiments were performed by strictly following the guidelines and regulations from the Laboratory Animal Centre of Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). In brief, a balb/c nude mouse was subcutaneously injected with 100 μL of the R-GQDs and O-GQDs aqueous solution (1 mg/mL) at the injection site after being anesthetized by intraperitoneal injection of 1% pentobarbital. The FL images were taken under an excitation light of 530 nm and an emission filter of 620 nm. The animal fluorescence imaging was conducted using IVIS Spectrum CT in vivo imaging system, and the exposure time was 1.0 s for all fluorescence images.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a Tecnai G20 transmission electron microscope at 200 kV. The morphology of the GQDs were observed by atomic force microscope (AFM, Asylum Research Cypher S) operating at the tapping mode. Raman scattering spectra were obtained using Horiba Jobin Yvon HR800 spectrometer with 532 nm laser. The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded using a Rigaku D/Max 2550 X-ray diffractometer with Cu-Ka radiation (γ = 0.15418 nm) in the range of 10–70° at room temperature. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out on an ESCALAB 250 Xi (Thermo Scientific, MA, USA) using non-monochromatized Mg-Kα X-ray as the excitation source. The binding energies for the samples were calibrated by setting the measured binding energy of C 1s to 284.60 eV. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) experiment was carried out on a Nicolet IS50 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer and used a wavenumber range of 400–4000 cm−1. The samples were prepared as pellets together with KBr. The photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) was recorded using a Shimadzu RF-5301 Fluorescence spectrophotometer. The time-resolved fluorescence spectra were collected with Edinburgh FLS980 steady state/transient fluorescence spectrometer. The linear sweep voltammetry (Chenhua Electrochemical Station) was conducted at scan rate of 5 mV/s in 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution. The electron spin resonance (ESR) signals were collected on a Bruker A300 spectrometer at room temperature using a 300 W Xe lamp (Beijing Perfectlight Co. Ltd., PLS-SXE300D) as light source. The QYs of the GQDs were measured by using three references of Rhodamine 6G in water, Rhodamine B in water and quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4, where the QY of the three references were 95%, 31% and 58%, respectively. The calculation equation is given below:
∅ s
=
∅ r
×
I s
I r
×
A r
A s
×
n s
2
n r
2
represent the QY, emission peak area, integrated absorption intensity and refractive index of GQDs solutions, respectively. ‘
∅ s
’ represent the QY of test substance, ‘
∅ r
’ represent the QY of reference. ‘
I s
’ represent the integrated absorption intensity of test substance, ‘
I r
’ represent the integrated absorption intensity of reference. ‘
A s
’ represent the emission peak area of test substance, ‘
A r
’ represent the emission peak area of reference. ‘
n s
’ represent the refractive index of test substance, ‘
n r
’ represent the refractive index of reference.

Oxidized GQDs were synthesized via the improved AOP reaction using graphite oxides as the precursors, during which the multi-layer graphite oxides were cut into 0D graphene quantum dots, shown in Scheme 1 . In details, the GO flakes are rich in oxygen groups and could be dispersed in water. When the Fe3+ and H2O2 were added in the GO solution, the dark GO solution turned light brown (Fig. S1), accompanied by a large amount of bubbles which can be assigned to oxygen. After the hydrothermal reaction, pale-yellow solution was obtained with a few maroon and black precipitates on the bottom which could be assigned to graphite and Fe2O3 (Fig. S2, Table S1).

By the catalysis of Fe3+/Fe2+ in acid solution, H2O2 could be dissociated into hydroxyl radicals (·OH). To prove the existence of hydroxyl radicals, the ESR characterization has been used, and the patterns demonstrated that ·OH could be produced when the AOP reagent existed in the system (Fig. S3). The ·OH of strong oxidative ability could act as a cutting agent by attacking the defective carbon atoms linked with oxygen-containing functional group serving as the reaction sites [16,20]. Then, the oxidation process would lead to the breaking of carbon–carbon bonds and generation of small organic molecules and carbon dioxide [16]. Hence, the large π-conjugated skeleton with micro-meter lateral size was cut into small oxidized graphene quantum dots [20,21]. Besides, the high hydrothermal temperature could reduce a small amount of the graphite oxides to insoluble graphite. Due to the addition of excess H2O2, the iron ions were converted to Fe(OH)3 which was then decomposed into Fe2O3 precipitates under the high hydrothermal temperature. The two components constituted for the precipitates in the product. The average yield of F-GQDs from graphite oxide powders was about 60%.

The reaction condition has been compared with some previously reported data, as shown in Table 1
[22–29]. In these literatures, the introduction of hard-to-remove inorganic salts and acids was unavoidable for some traditional oxidation-cutting methods, which was time and cost-consuming. For other strategies utilizing new cutting agents, it is found to be hard to achieve a balance among high yield, easy post-treatment and uniform size distribution. In contrast, the advanced oxidation process is firstly a well-developed sustainable and industrializable production procedure of practical basis. Meanwhile, it is obvious that the improved AOP-driven stripping strategy for synthesizing GQDs in our work was efficient, easily operated, and environmentally friendly. In details, the average yield calculated from 20 trials reached up to 60% and the average size distribution of the well-crystalline dots was measured to be ~3.7 nm, which demonstrated the repeatability of the method and good quality of the product. The cheap and safe precursors (total price of the precursors: 0.42 US dollars/g, Table S2), rapid preparation process (~8 h) and easy post-treatment (simple filter) were also prominent advantages for practical production.

The morphology and size distribution of the F-GQDs is studied using TEM and AFM showed in Fig. 1 (a–c). It can be seen that the quantum dots have a good dispersity with uniform size of 3.7 ± 0.6 nm. As shown in the HRTEM image (Fig. 1(b)), the lattice fringe is measured to be 0.24 nm, corresponding to the crystal structure of graphene (1120) planes. In Fig. 1(c), the AFM image and a height profile along the yellow line suggest that the thickness of the F-GQDs is between 1.1 and 2.0 nm, which indicates its lamellar structure between 3 and 5 layers. Moreover, the XRD patterns of the precursor (graphite oxide) and F-GQDs is compared in Fig. 1(d). In the XRD pattern of GO, there is a (0 0 1) peak at 10° and a (1 0 0) peak at 42°. For F-GQDs, the (0 0 1) peak at 10.5° slightly right-shifted and the (1 0 1) peak at 42° vanishes, suggesting that the graphitization degree of F-GQDs is higher than that of GO. A broad diffraction peak centered at 28°corresponding to the (0 0 2) plane of graphite is also observed. The Raman spectra of F-GQDs (Fig. 1(e)) show two peaks at 1334 and 1577 cm−1, corresponding to the disordered structures (D band) and the graphitic carbon domains (G band), respectively. The intensity ratios (ID/IG) are 1.06, which is significantly lower than that of GO (ID/IG = 1.18), indicating an increase in the size of sp2­domains, which is consistent with the XRD results.

The structure of the GQDs is characterized by FT-IR and XPS spectra. Fig. 1(f) shows the FT-IR spectra of F-GQDs and graphite oxides. The peak at 1065 and 1402 cm−1 is assigned to the C-OH vibration and strong vibration of OCO, respectively. The strong vibration at 1626 cm−1 is attributed to the CC bond. Besides, two obvious bands at 3400 cm−1 and 3175 cm−1 are assigned to the OH stretching vibration of adsorbed water molecules shown in the FT-IR spectrum of F-GQDs. The peaks confirmed the rich organic group encapsulating outside the carbogenic cores. The XPS spectra show strong signals for C 1s and O 1s. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum (Fig. S4(a)) displays a signal of CC bonding at 284.6 eV and C-O, COOH bonding at 285.7 eV and 288.9 eV, respectively. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. S4(b)) then displays the presence of COH and OCO bonding at 531.7 and 532.7 eV, respectively. The XPS analysis results suggest that some oxygen-rich functional groups formed on the surface of F-GQDs under AOP reaction.

The optical properties of F-GQDs and graphite oxide are studied by UV–vis and PL spectra, showed in Fig. 1(g, h) and Fig. S5. One main peak at 230 nm derived from the π-π* transition of CC and CC bonds in the sp2 hybrid region shows both in the spectra of F-GQDs and GO. For graphite oxides, a broad shoulder peak around 303 nm is due to n-π* transition of the CO bond in sp3 hybrid regions, while a characteristic abroad absorption peak appeared at 400 nm in the spectrum of F-GQDs, which is assigned to the n-π* transition and the contribution of surface state. The optimal PL excitation and emission peaks are 320 nm and 465 nm, respectively. At 320 nm excitation, the fluorescence quantum efficiency could reach 12.9%. However, the PL emission of graphite oxide is barely detectable even by varying the excitation wavelengths (Fig. S5, dotted curve). The PL spectra of F-GQDs exhibit excitation-independent property showing same emission peaks centered at 465 nm under different excitation wavelengths, and the single exponential lifetime of the fluorescence is 8 ns (Fig. 1(h)), which may be due to the existence of excessive defect on the surface states of GQDs. The PL decay curve of graphite oxides show that the material has a dual exponential lifetime of the fluorescence is 0.9 ns, which is extremely short (Fig. 1(h), blue curve). It is because of that the introduction of rich surface carboxyl groups on the surface of F-GQDs could induce significant local deformation, thus leading to an obvious increase in the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the PL emission spectrum [10].

PL-tunable GQDs with emission peaks ranging from blue to red region via modification of F-GQDs have been synthesized by using five different nitrogen sources. Two main strategies are exploited, including altering the surface states of F-GQDs by conjugating with polyaromatic rings and modification of amine functional groups. The Raman spectra of the five samples (Fig. S6(a)) display two peaks centered at 1348 cm−1 (D band) and 1587 cm−1 (G band). The intensity ratios (ID/IG) are 1.14, 1.12, 1.10, 1.06 and 1.02 for B­, G­, Y­, O-, and R­GQDs, respectively, suggesting a gradual increase in the size of sp2­domains. In Fig. S6(b), the FT-IR spectra show that these GQDs have abundant functional groups, including OH group at 3450 cm−1, NH at 3167 cm−1, CH at 3167 cm−1, OCO at 1399 cm−1 and COH at 1157 cm−1. Interestingly, it is found that the stretching vibration intensity of C-N at 1596 cm−1 increased gradually, while the stretching vibration of CN at 1696 cm−1 increased significantly from B-GQDs to R-GQDs. The phenomena indicate that different types of nitrogen sources affect the content of graphitic nitrogen atoms in the carbon skeleton. By comparison with the Raman spectra of the five samples (Fig. S6(a)), it is demonstrated that there is a gradual increase in the size of sp2­domains from B-GQDs to R-GQDs. In Fig. 2 (a–e), TEM images and particle size distribution diagrams show that these GQDs with uniform size are well dispersed, and the modification process did not lead to any agglomeration of the quantum dots. The average particle sizes of B-, G-, Y-, O- and R-GQDs are ~2.7, 3.4, 4.1, 4.5 and 5.1 nm, respectively. HR-TEM images show that all samples have clear lattice fringes with a space of 0.24 nm which is assigned to the (1120) plane of graphitic carbon. The AFM images shown in Fig. 2(f–j) reveal the thickness of different PL-tunable GQDs, indicating 2–6 graphene layers for the five samples. In addition, the suspension of GQDs also showed extra-high stability even after being kept for months in air at room temperature.

The XPS full spectra in Fig. S7 confirm the existence of C, N and O elements, by displaying three typical peaks for C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s at 285, 400, and 531 eV, respectively. In the high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. S8), the C 1s band could be deconvolved into four binding energy peaks centered at 284.6, 285.5, 286 and 288.3 eV, which could be attributed to CC/CC, CN, CO and COOH species. The N1s spectra all show three peaks at 398.9, 399.8 and 400.9 eV, assigned to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively [30]. The O1s signals then show three broad peaks at the binding energies of ca. 531, 532 and 532.7 eV [31], which correspond to the OCOH, COH and CO species. The atomic percentages of different elements are listed in Table S3. It is found that the atomic ratio between nitrogen and carbon increases from 0.05 to 0.27 as the N content increases from 4.33 to 17.28% for B-GQDs to R-GQDs. The data confirms the decrease of the graphitization degree in the carbon structure.

Furtherly, the percentages of different bonding composition calculated by XPS analysis is listed in Table 2 . It is found that the content of sp2-carbon decreased from 63.54% (B-GQDs) to 59.26% (R-GQDs), and the content of C-N bonding increased from 5% (B-GQDs) to 27.33% (R-GQDs), both demonstrative of the decrease of graphitization degree. The content of oxygen-containing groups was shown to decrease from 31.46% (B-GQDs) to 13.41% (R-GQDs). In addition, the proportion of graphitic nitrogen increased gradually, and the content of pyridinic nitrogen and pyrrolic nitrogen both decreased obviously. The sp2-graphitic nitrogen is an important part of the structure contributing to the bandgap absorption and providing an extra electron to the conjugated system [31]. Pyrrolic nitrogen could also donate one pair of electrons to the conductivity of π-system which can improve the PL quantum yields of GQDs, while the pyridinic nitrogen usually has an electron-withdrawing nature which is opposite to the effect of graphitic and pyrrolic nitrogen [31–35].

The optimal PL emission peaks are located at 442, 511, 578, 591 and 620 nm, respectively. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the five samples shown in Fig. 3 (a-e) reveal significant absorption in both UV and visible regions. In the UV region, two peaks were observed centered at 234 and 280 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transitions of the CC and CN bonds in the carbon core, respectively [36]. Their absorption intensities in the UV region increase from B-GQDs to R-GQDs, demonstrating that the content of nitro-substituted aromatic rings in the carbon core gradually increases. However, in the visible-light region, five samples showed different absorption bands at 381, 410, 450, 518 and 580 nm (shown in Fig. S9), respectively, indicating that these samples have different surface states [37,38]. The relative QYs of B-, G-, Y-, O- and R-GQDs were calculated by reference method at their optimum excitation wavelengths, which are 18.3%, 41.4%, 38.3%, 37.5% and 18.7% (Table S4), respectively. Meanwhile, with the decrease of CO and OCOH groups in GQDs, the recombination of electrons and holes could be decreased, since the carriers are easily captured by the oxygen­related surface defects, which leads to a gradually narrowed FWHM from 90 to 64 nm in the PL emission spectra from B-GQDs to R-GQDs (Table S4). The phenomenon is similar to that of the typical surface-states-controlled GQDs [8,39].

Similar to many other reported GQDs, their PL emission peaks demonstrate excitation-dependent proper-ties, which is consistent with the F-GQDs [32,40]. Their time-resolved PL decay curves are also measured, and the results (Fig. 3(f); Table S5) show that the curve could be processed via a double exponential formula, including a transient component τ1 (about 1 ns) and a long-term component τ2 (about 13 ns) which represent the recombination of the initially populated core state and surface state, respectively. Obviously, from B-GQDs to R-GQDs, with the decrease of the average lifetime from 9.8 to 2.6 ns, the τ1 ratio increases from 32% to 96%. Meanwhile, the PL is found to be red-shifted with the increase of the size of GQDs. These results confirm the role of the size of GQDs in decreasing the radiation lifetime of the PL-tunable GQDs [41].

To study the PL mechanism, the band structures of different GQDs were calculated via linear sweep voltammetry to determine the conduction band minimum (CBM) and valence band maximum (VBM), shown in Fig. 4 (a-b). The CBMs of B-GQDs, G-GQDs, Y-GQDs, O-GQDs and R-GQDs are −1.54, −1.09, −1.05, −0.94 and −0.89 eV (relative to Ag/AgCl), respectively, and their corresponding VBMs are 1.52, 1.41, 1.30, 1.23 and 1.16 eV. The bandgaps of B-GQDs, G-GQDs, Y-GQDs, O-GQDs and R-GQDs are determined as 3.06, 2.50, 2.35, 2.17 and 2.05 eV, respectively Apparently seen from the energy diagram in Fig. 4(c), when the PL emission is red shifted, the corresponding band gap gradually decreases with the ascend of HOMO level and the descend of LUMO level. Generally, the PL emission of carbon dots could be tuned by their band structure including the sp2-conjugated size and surface states [8,17]. In our work, the red shift of PL emission is due to the bandgap narrowing which is indeed a result of the synergistic effect of larger π-electron system and higher content of graphite nitrogen. Shown in the above characterization results, the Raman and XPS analysis indicate that the amounts of polyaromatic-ring structures and amine functional groups increase and the graphitization degree decrease from B-GQDs to R-GQDs. Then, the TEM and AFM images demonstrate that the particle size increases with the red shift of PL emission. Conjugating the F-GQDs with polyaromatic rings can effectively enlarge the π-conjugated sp2-carbon network, thus decreasing the LUMO (π*) level in a ring-size-dependent manner and narrowing the bandgap [17,42]. Meanwhile, increasing the percentage of graphitic nitrogen can result in a bandgap reduction by injecting one excess electron into the unoccupied π* orbital [32,43,44]. Attributed to the π* electron, graphite N has a strong N-doping effect, which would contribute to the formation of deep levels in the bandgap and decrease the energy of transitions [44,45]. Therefore, appropriate modification of F-GQDs with different nitrogen polyaromatic molecules can continuously ad-just the HOMO/LUMO energy levels, leading to the shift of PL emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioimaging and white light-emitting composite film
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the multi-color photoluminescence and strong PL intensity, it is expected that the PL-tunable GQDs could be utilized in the application of bioimaging for HepG2 cells. The cytotoxicity of the blue, green and red GQDs was firstly assessed, as shown in Fig. S10. After 48 h’ incubation of HepG2 cells with the three GQDs at a concentration ranging from 0 to 500 μg/mL, the results display that all samples exhibited extremely low cytotoxicity and all cell viabilities are above 90%.

For fluorescence probes, the cellular imaging capabilities were investigated by incubating the HepG2 cells with each of the three GQDs for 4 h and then irradiated under confocal microscope. The confocal micrographs show different color emission in HepG2 cells, that is blue emission of B-GQDs, green emission of G-GQDs and red emission of R-GQDs, respectively (Fig. 5 (a–f)). It has also been found that the fluorescence is mainly located in the cytoplasmic region, indicating that all three GQDs have penetrated into the cell through the cell membrane and could remain stable fluorescence signal over long incubation times. The cell viability and bio-imaging assays demonstrate that the obtained PL-tunable GQDs of good biocompatibility can be used as safe and efficient fluorescence nanoprobes. Furtherly, the mouse imaging was performed in vivo. In Fig. 5(h–i), a strong fluorescence signal at the injection site could be observed with an excitation light of 530 nm and an emission filter of 620 nm, implying that the red fluorescence of R-GQDs and O-GQDs are even able to penetrate a healthy mouse skin and tissues efficiently. During the experimental courses, no animals showed any signs of an acute toxicological response. All these results confirm that our GQDs can be used as an effective luminescence probe for bioimaging both in vitro and in vivo.

Another application of the PL-tunable GQDs is to prepare GQDs/PVA films with color­tunable emission (Fig. 6 ). The CIE color coordinates (0.33, 0.34) of the pure white light-emitting GQDs/PVA composite film were obtained under 365 nm ultraviolet light after mixing B-GQDs, G-GQDs and R-GQDs at a weight ratio of 2:2:3. The white-emitting fluorescent film has a QY of 24%, which is quite remarkable for white-light emitting GQDs. As shown in Fig. 6(b), the stability of the composite film was tested under the irradiation of a 150 W Xe lamp with a center wavelength of 420 nm for 24 h. It is found that the PL intensity of the white light-emitting films slightly decreased, displaying good photostability of the film. For the emission peak of the white-light emitting GQDs/PVA composite film, three different emission bands are observed in Fig. 6(a-b), which suggests that there is no significant energy transfer or self-absorption within the GQDs-based mixture [8,40,46]. Therefore, the white-light emitting GQDs/PVA composite films are potential materials for designing lighting devices in the future.

In our study, a thermal-driven AOP strategy was exploited to efficiently stripping large cheap graphite oxides into GQDs of uniform and ultra-small size with an average yield of 60%. The obtained GQDs were furtherly modified with different nitrogen-containing resources to tune the band structure, successfully achieving PL-tunable GQDs with blue, green, yellow, orange and red emissions. It is found that the obtained GQDs have an average size distribution of 2–5 nm and the corresponding AFM images determined their lamellar structure of 2–6 layers. Meanwhile, the structure and morphology analysis results demonstrate that the average size of the GQDs gradually increases from B-GQDs to R-GQDs, which is due to the modification of different size of polyaromatic moieties. Furtherly, the content of graphite nitrogen also increases with the red shift of PL emission. The two strategies including expanding the size of π-conjugated sp2-carbon system and introducing more graphite nitrogen into the carbon skeleton both contribute to the band gap narrowing, which is also confirmed by the linear sweep voltammetry. The band gap narrowing directly leads to the red shift of PL emission. The precise bandgap engineering offers GQDs more possibilities in various optical applications. The PL-tunable GQDs are demonstrative of good biocompatibility, which could be used as excellent fluorescent probes for bioimaging both in vitro and in vivo. Moreover, the GQDs were mixed with PVA to prepare white-light-emitting composite film, which owns high potential in developing WLED devices. The AOP-driven stripping strategy exhibits great potentials in industrial applications, and might provide a new vision for preparing smart polymer or carbon nitride quantum dots of high-yield in the future.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We greatly appreciate the financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51702212, 51802195), Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (18511110600, 19ZR1435200), Innovation Program of Shanghai Municipal Education Commission (2019-01-07-00-07-E00015), Program of Shanghai Academic Research Leader (19XD1422900).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2020.124285.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
